regions.label: text
regions.text: SATURDAY, APRIL 12, 1924

regions.label: text
regions.text: New York, April 11.—Justice probably will take its course and mete out the proper decision in the case of Patrolman Howard C. Clancy (white), a judge of the special service squad, who was killed early morning of Feb. 23, deliberately took his gun and broke the glass door of Neville Avenue, near the Neville Avenue, through which he climbed and firedProminently into a crowd of killing David Harris, 48, 503 E. 125d St.

regions.label: text
regions.text: Clancy is charged with manslaughter in House in the homicide court in $10,000 for examination. It is said that Clancy was not wearing a tinting in the Harlem district he heard pushed. He entered and engaged in a free-for-all fight, during which he was cut up. According to Assistant District Attorney Rynan, Clancy's economics are not well. Clancy was accused while intoxicated and demanded whiskey from Lew Allon, the bartender, in the place the plain clothes police officer was ambushed and threatening language. Finally he was forced out the side door, becoming more angered at the execution. Becoming more angered at the execution, he entered the glass in the door and climbed back into the place and began shooting. Hartley fully playing cards in the place at the chest in the chest and Paul Lightfoot, 42, W. 324 N. St., was in the arm, after the occurrence the malicious people police had been dangerously slushed during a fight with a score of 12. The fact that Clancy is charged with should explain the case satisfactorily.

regions.text: The Carlton Ave. branch has had a good ment and friends interested in the work to bring in donations in order to close the ment and friends interested in the work required last week this time. around the month of April, the branch will be open at the Brooklyn Ave. A will open for donations until April 15. The branch will be open in good, in view of the fact that only a few hundred dollars are needed for the donations appreciated at this time. The branch will meet at 7 o'clock at the Carlton club will meet at 7 o'clock at the branch. A good old-time lean supper will be served in Brooklyn Ave. a big tge at Brooks like during the month in support of the community in life are invited to the meeting and supper Saturday evening. Art league meet on Wednesday evening. Many art enthusiasts of both sides. The R. I. M. Mercery Library association will meet in Brooklyn Ave. all friends of the former secretary of the Carlton Ave. branch are invited to have a reference library credible in every respect. Many pikes of the branch promised a book for each of the recent annual fairs are working hard to get all money in on outstanding tickets. Performers kindly report same to their chairman as soon as possible. The Catin will deliver the fifth of his talks on the second afternoon. The puiddle is invited.

MATT HENSON SPEAKER.
AT-ST. MARK'S LYCEUM
Matthew Henson, only survivor of the accompanied Admiral Perry on his expedition to the Pacific, spoke at St. Mark's Lyceum Sunday afternoon. April 6 was the anniversary of his death, vividly the hardships and experiences the job enthused only Hanson and Pearl lived to discover. The explorer was a master of music and literature, a course. In addition to the address an interesting musical and literary program, Thomas in charge. John E. Robinson, with a purse by the members and friends as a token of apreciation for the organization.
